prompt: Translate the following English sentence into French, Japanese, and Arabic, and add a brief note on any phrase that was tricky to render faithfully: "The silence between us said more than any argument could have."
response:
French  
« Le silence entre nous en disait plus que n’aurait pu le faire aucune dispute. »

Japanese  
私たちの間の沈黙は、どんな言い争いよりも多くを語っていた。  
(Watashitachi no aida no chinkoku wa, donna iiarai yori mo ōku o katatte ita.)

Arabic  
السكوت بيننا قال أكثر ممّا كان يستطيع أيّ جدال أن يقول.  
(as-sukūt baynanā qāla akthar mim-mā kāna yastaṭīʿu ʾayyu jadāl ʾan yaqūl.)

Tricky bit  
The English idiom “said more” (silence actively “speaks”) resists word-for-word translation.  
- French: the verb dire is still used, but wrapped in the impersonal “en dire plus.”  
- Japanese: 語っていた (katatte ita) lets silence “narrate,” a natural metaphor.  
- Arabic: قال (qāla) is normally audible speech; here it is deliberately stretched to keep the personification without sounding odd.
